Pricing

Product analytics your whole team can use

Acme gives product, growth, and customer success teams one dependable view of how people discover features, complete important workflows, and return to the product. Teams can start with a small event plan, review clear definitions, and expand their tracking as questions become more specific. Every dashboard keeps its source events visible, so readers can check what a metric means before they use it in planning or a weekly review.

Dashboards that answer questions

Build funnels for onboarding, compare retention across account cohorts, and inspect the paths people take before conversion. Saved reports include ownership, update history, and plain language notes. Those details reduce duplicate analysis and make it easier for a new teammate to understand why a measure exists. Filters are consistent across reports, and a shared date range keeps review meetings focused on the same period.

Designed for careful decisions

Alerts highlight meaningful changes without treating every small fluctuation as an emergency. Teams can annotate releases, campaigns, and incidents, then compare those events with product behavior. Access controls separate private account data from company-wide trends. Exported tables retain metric names and time windows, which helps analysts reproduce a result in their preferred notebook. Acme also documents sampling and late-arriving events so stakeholders can judge the limits of a result instead of assuming false precision.

Implementation guides cover browser, server, and mobile tracking. A validation screen shows recent events before they affect a production report, while schema checks flag missing fields and unexpected types. Customer success teams can follow account adoption without reading raw event logs. Product managers can compare feature use by plan, region, or signup month. Growth teams can connect acquisition sources to activation while keeping identity rules explicit. Together, these workflows provide enough context for routine decisions without hiding the evidence behind a single score.
